ac:rich-text-body.p: Overview of observing time allocation, project types, and access policies for SKA science operations.

p: Proposals are issued 6 months before each observing cycle. Time is allocated based on scientific merit and technical feasibility. The allocation process maintains proportionality to member state contributions to the Observatory.
The allocation process employs dual anonymous assessment combining distributed peer review with thematic panel review. This approach ensures fair and rigorous evaluation of all proposals.

Standard observing projects available to the community. During Cycles 0 and 1, PI projects operate under "shared risk" with limited performance guarantees. From Cycle 2 onwards, they transition to standard operations with full guarantees.

Rapid-response observing time allocated by the Director for time-sensitive targets. DDT follows the same shared risk/standard operations timeline as PI projects, ensuring consistent operational readiness.

The most ambitious observing programmes, requiring significantly more observing time distributed over multiple cycles. Key Science Projects are expected to commence from Cycle 3 onwards and represent strategic science priorities for the Observatory.
The SKA provides an integrated suite of web-based and command-line tools to support proposal submission, observation execution, and scheduling:

ul.li: Proposal Handling Tool (PHT) — Web-based interface with dual views. The Submission Tool view is designed for astronomers preparing proposals; the Handling Tool view supports reviewers in the evaluation process.
Observation Execution Tool — Manages the execution of approved observing programmes.
Scheduling and Execution Tools — Coordinate observation scheduling with real-time execution support.
Multiple User Interfaces — GUIs and CLIs available to support the workflows of different user roles, from casual observers to advanced power users.

p: All observing data obtained through PI-led projects are subject to a standard proprietary period. During this time, the Principal Investigator has exclusive rights to the data for scientific publication and analysis.
Following the proprietary period, all data becomes part of the public archive and is made available to the broader community for scientific use.
The SKA is committed to open science principles and ensures equality of access to archive data regardless of the researcher's location, institution, or member state affiliation.

ac:rich-text-body.p: For detailed information on data access procedures, computational resources, and user credentials, consult the About the SRCnet page.
User support is available through regional centres. Contact your local centre for assistance with proposal preparation, observation planning, or data access.
